lead.description: Project the aircraft passing overhead onto your ceiling in real time, from an RTL-SDR — with a live sky layer (sun, moon, stars, ISS) and where each plane is headed.

hoodRepo.description: 9Drive is a storage gateway web app for connecting multiple Google Drive accounts into one virtual storage dashboard. Users can connect Google Drive accounts, track quota, upload files, organize files with virtual folders, preview files, and let the backend route uploads to the Drive account with enough free space.

ideaRepo.description: Run Claude Design locally as an Agent Skill — Cursor, Claude Code & more. Produce polished UI mockups, prototypes, decks & wireframes as self-contained HTML, without claude.ai/design. Best with Opus 4.8.

resurrection.description: An agent-managed museum exhibit, built in Rust with Gajae-Code / LazyCodex — developed and maintained with no human intervention.

signalArticle:
This week, `cpaczek/skylight` has captured the GitHub community's attention, rapidly accumulated stars for its unique offering. It transforms flight tracking into an immersive, ambient experience: real-time projections of overhead aircraft, directly onto your ceiling. Powered by an affordable RTL-SDR receiver, a Raspberry Pi, and a projector, `skylight` integrates a dynamic celestial layer—sun, moon, stars, and even the ISS—creating a living window to the sky. Its instant appeal lies in making the invisible visible, turning raw radio signals into a personal, captivating art installation that resonates right now.

The project exists at a fascinating intersection of trends: the democratization of specialized data like ADS-B signals, the power of physical computing for creative endeavors, and the growing demand for personalized ambient displays. Beyond simple novelty, `skylight` exemplifies how accessible hardware platforms, coupled with sophisticated software, can elevate raw data into compelling interactive art. It represents a broader movement blending digital insights seamlessly into physical environments, transforming complex aerial traffic into intuitive, beautiful visualizations.

For developers and tech observers, `skylight` offers more than just a cool gadget; it's a masterclass in full-stack integration. From hardware interfacing with an RTL-SDR on a Raspberry Pi, through real-time data processing, to a slick, responsive TypeScript and React frontend, it showcases robust architectural choices and the tangible impact of open-source tooling. It proves powerful, innovative user experiences are achievable with readily available components. It's a compelling case study for exploring IoT, data visualization, and translating complex data into engaging, accessible realities.
hoodArticle:
9Drive distinguishes itself with an architectural approach centered on a sophisticated virtualization layer over disparate Google Drive accounts, acting as an intelligent storage gateway rather than a mere aggregator. A key design is the backend's dynamic routing of file uploads. This involves continuous quota monitoring across all user-linked Google Drive instances, followed by proxying uploads to the account with sufficient available space. This abstraction provides a unified virtual file system, allowing the frontend to interact with a singular, consistent API endpoint. The client remains unaware of the underlying multi-account distribution or storage allocation, simplifying development and significantly enhancing user experience.

The most compelling problem 9Drive solves at a code level is the seamless, automated management of distributed file storage and intelligent resource allocation across multiple Google Drive quotas. Its implementation stands out by transparently allocating storage for new uploads without user intervention. This necessitates a robust data model mapping user-defined virtual folder structures to specific files and accounts. Critically, a smart upload service intercepts requests, consults aggregated quota data, and intelligently dispatches the file to the optimally available Drive account, effectively functioning as a self-optimizing storage orchestrator.
ideaArticle:
Imagine having a super-smart design assistant living right on your computer, ready to create beautiful websites, app interfaces, or presentations for you instantly. That's the core idea behind this project. Instead of relying on a distant online service that uses powerful AI like Claude Design, this tool brings that intelligence directly to your machine. It translates your design concepts into ready-to-use, polished user interface mockups, interactive prototypes, or even presentation decks, all packaged as standard web files (HTML). It’s like having the full power of an AI design studio at your fingertips, completely offline and without sending your ideas to a third-party website.

This addresses a significant need for speed, privacy, and accessibility in design. Many individuals and small teams struggle to quickly visualize product ideas, create prototypes, or simply get professional-looking designs without hiring a designer or subscribing to expensive, complex software. Founders can rapidly mock up new features, product managers can iterate on user experiences in real-time, and developers can generate attractive interfaces without needing design expertise. It’s perfect for anyone who values maintaining privacy for sensitive projects, wants instant visual output, or needs to bridge the gap between an idea and a tangible user interface, all while keeping control of their data and creative process on their own terms.

resurrectionArticle:
ultraworkers/claw-code was nothing short of revolutionary when it debuted in 2026. A Rust-based agent-managed museum exhibit, its true significance lay in its development: the entire application, from concept to ongoing maintenance, was built by Gajae-Code and LazyCodex with no human intervention. It stood as an audacious proof-of-concept for truly autonomous software creation, fundamentally reshaping perceptions of AI as the sole architect of complex systems.

Years after its last push, claw-code is surging back into relevance. The current ecosystem, brimming with advanced autonomous agents and code generation, has finally matured to meet ultraworkers' vision. What was once an experimental, almost prophetic statement about AI's potential is now becoming a practical benchmark. Developers are revisiting this dormant project not just as a historical curiosity, but as a foundational blueprint, eager to apply its "no human intervention" ethos to today's self-evolving software landscape.
